# File-mode differential cases.
#
# Format per case (one case per non-empty paragraph):
#   SETUP: <SQL fed to `sqlite3 fix.db` to build the fixture>
#   QUERY: <SQL fed to BOTH `sqlite3 fix.db` and `sqlite0 -file fix.db` for diff>
#
# Each blank-line-separated block is one case. Lines starting with `#` are
# ignored. SETUP runs once via the system sqlite3 CLI to materialise the
# .db file; QUERY then runs against both engines and the outputs must match
# byte-for-byte.

SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y'), (3, 'z');
QUERY: SELECT * FROM t

SETUP: CREATE TABLE n(x INTEGER); INSERT INTO n VALUES (10), (20), (30), (40);
QUERY: SELECT x FROM n WHERE x > 15

SETUP: CREATE TABLE p(name TEXT, age INTEGER); INSERT INTO p VALUES ('alice', 30), ('bob', 25), ('carol', 35);
QUERY: SELECT name FROM p WHERE age >= 30 ORDER BY name

SETUP: CREATE TABLE k(c); INSERT INTO k VALUES ('apple'), ('banana'), ('cherry');
QUERY: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM k

SETUP: CREATE TABLE pair(a, b); INSERT INTO pair VALUES (1, 2), (3, 4), (5, 6);
QUERY: SELECT a + b FROM pair

# Multi-page B-tree: 500 rows of moderate width force the table B-tree to
# split into multiple leaves (and likely an interior root). Exercises
# btree_walk.TableLeafWalker end-to-end on a real sqlite3-emitted tree.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE big(n INTEGER, label TEXT); WITH RECURSIVE c(i) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT i+1 FROM c WHERE i < 500) INSERT INTO big SELECT i, 'row-' || i FROM c;
QUERY: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM big

SETUP: CREATE TABLE big(n INTEGER, label TEXT); WITH RECURSIVE c(i) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT i+1 FROM c WHERE i < 500) INSERT INTO big SELECT i, 'row-' || i FROM c;
QUERY: SELECT n FROM big WHERE n = 1 OR n = 250 OR n = 500 ORDER BY n

# Two tables → JOIN through both BtreeCursor instances.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE a(id, v); CREATE TABLE b(id, w); INSERT INTO a VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y'); INSERT INTO b VALUES (1, 100), (2, 200);
QUERY: SELECT a.v, b.w FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.id ORDER BY a.id

# Aggregate over a real-file table.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE g(grp TEXT, val INTEGER); INSERT INTO g VALUES ('a', 1), ('a', 2), ('b', 3), ('b', 4), ('b', 5);
QUERY: SELECT grp, SUM(val) FROM g GROUP BY grp ORDER BY grp

# NULL handling in record decode. Both engines walk a table B-tree in
# rowid (= insertion) order without an explicit ORDER BY, so the output
# matches without referencing the rowid pseudo-column (which sqlite0
# doesn't expose yet — Iter25.B.5 scope).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE n2(a, b); INSERT INTO n2 VALUES (1, NULL), (NULL, 2), (3, 4);
QUERY: SELECT a, b FROM n2

# Real numbers (8-byte IEEE 754 BE).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE r(x REAL); INSERT INTO r VALUES (1.5), (-2.25), (3.14159);
QUERY: SELECT x FROM r ORDER BY x

# BLOB column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE bl(payload BLOB); INSERT INTO bl VALUES (x'deadbeef'), (x'00ff');
QUERY: SELECT length(payload) FROM bl

# Iter26.A.1 INSERT cases (write path). The harness clones the fixture,
# runs MUTATE through both engines on independent copies, then runs
# VERIFY against both and diffs. PRAGMA integrity_check on the
# sqlite0-mutated copy catches byte-format corruption that VERIFY alone
# might not surface.

# Single INSERT into an empty file-mode table.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x')
VERIFY: SELECT * FROM t

# Multi-row INSERT in one statement.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'a'), (2, 'b'), (3, 'c')
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# INSERT into a non-empty table — rowids must continue past max.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (10), (20)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (30), (40)
VERIFY: SELECT a FROM t

# Mixed-type INSERT (NULL, REAL, TEXT on the same row). BLOB literals
# (`x'...'` syntax) aren't accepted by the sqlite0 parser yet — that's
# unrelated to the file-mode write path and tracked separately.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE m(i, f, t)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO m VALUES (NULL, 3.14, 'hi')
VERIFY: SELECT i, f, t FROM m

# Two separate INSERT statements in one sqlite0 invocation.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2)
VERIFY: SELECT x FROM t

# Iter26.A.2.a DELETE cases (file-mode rebuild-page).

# DELETE without WHERE — drop everything from a populated table.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3)
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t

# DELETE WHERE matching a subset.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y'), (3, 'z')
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE a = 2
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# DELETE WHERE matching no rows — table contents and on-disk bytes unchanged.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (10), (20)
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE a = 999
VERIFY: SELECT a FROM t

# DELETE then re-SELECT after a mixed INSERT+DELETE batch.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4)
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE x > 2
VERIFY: SELECT x FROM t

# Iter26.A.2.b UPDATE cases (file-mode rebuild-page).

# UPDATE without WHERE — rewrite every row.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET b = 'z'
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# UPDATE WHERE matching one row, single column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'old'), (2, 'old'), (3, 'old')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET b = 'new' WHERE a = 2
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# UPDATE multiple columns at once.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE p(name TEXT, age INTEGER); INSERT INTO p VALUES ('alice', 30), ('bob', 25)
MUTATE: UPDATE p SET name = 'BOB', age = 26 WHERE name = 'bob'
VERIFY: SELECT name, age FROM p ORDER BY name

# UPDATE WHERE matches no rows — file unchanged.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2)
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET a = 99 WHERE a = 999
VERIFY: SELECT a FROM t

# UPDATE growing the row's record (small int → larger int).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER); INSERT INTO t VALUES (5), (10)
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET n = 1000000 WHERE n = 5
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# UPDATE assignment-order: every RHS sees the OLD row. sqlite3 yields
# (11, 1) for (a + 10, a) because b's RHS reads the pre-update a.
# A naive in-place mutation between assignments would yield (11, 11).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 0)
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET a = a + 10, b = a
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# Iter26.A.3 file-mode CREATE TABLE cases. Both engines run the same
# MUTATE on independent file copies (each starting from the SETUP
# fixture); VERIFY then runs against both and the outputs must match.
# `PRAGMA integrity_check` on the sqlite0-mutated copy catches any
# byte-format divergence that VERIFY alone might not surface.
#
# Round-trip is skipped for these cases because VERIFY queries
# `sqlite_schema` directly, which sqlite0 doesn't yet expose as a
# user-queryable table (it only reads it internally during open).

# CREATE TABLE on an existing file, then SELECT confirms the schema
# is visible to sqlite3.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(a, b)
VERIFY: SELECT name FROM sqlite_schema WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name
SKIP_RT: 1

# CREATE + INSERT + SELECT round-trip through sqlite0; sqlite3 then
# reads the inserted rows.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(a, b); INSERT INTO u VALUES (1, 'hello'), (2, 'world')
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM u

# CREATE TABLE preserves column types in the stored sql column —
# sqlite3 reads the schema row's sql to drive its own typing.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(id INTEGER, name TEXT)
VERIFY: SELECT sql FROM sqlite_schema WHERE name='u'
SKIP_RT: 1

# Two CREATE TABLE statements in one batch — both must land and both
# must show up in sqlite_schema.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE a1(p); CREATE TABLE a2(q)
VERIFY: SELECT name FROM sqlite_schema WHERE type='table' AND name LIKE 'a%' ORDER BY name
SKIP_RT: 1

# Iter26.B.1 balance-deeper cases. Each MUTATE issues a single INSERT
# whose combined cell list overflows one leaf (~100 rows of ~50-byte
# text), forcing executeInsertFile down the balance-deeper path: the
# leaf root gets converted into an interior root pointing at two new
# leaf children, with the root_page number kept stable so the
# sqlite_schema entry remains valid. integrity_check on the
# sqlite0-mutated copy verifies the resulting B-tree is structurally
# valid for sqlite3, and the harness's sqlite0 round-trip read closes
# the loop (sqlite0 must re-read what it just wrote).

# Total row count after balance-deeper.

MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'all'
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t WHERE s = 'all'

# Iter26.C.1 read-side overflow chain. sqlite3 INSERTs a 6000-byte
# TEXT row (≥ X = 4049 on 4084 usable), which spills into one
# overflow chain page. sqlite0 must walk the chain on SELECT.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES (replace(hex(zeroblob(3000)), '0', 'a'))
QUERY: SELECT length(s), substr(s, 1, 5), substr(s, 5990, 10) FROM t

# Iter26.C.1: oversize row in a multi-row table — chain must be
# parsed alongside inline-only siblings.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'short'),(2, replace(hex(zeroblob(3500)), '0', 'a')),(3, 'tail')
QUERY: SELECT n, length(s) FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter26.C.2 INSERT-side overflow: a 6000-char string spills into one
# overflow chain page (P=6004 on 4084 usable → inline=487, spill=5517 →
# 2 chain pages). SETUP creates an empty table, MUTATE inserts the
# oversize row, VERIFY round-trips length/prefix/suffix through both
# engines + integrity_check.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES ('')
VERIFY: SELECT length(s), substr(s, 1, 5), substr(s, 5990, 10) FROM t

# Iter26.C.2: two oversize rows. Each takes M=487 inline bytes plus a
# 4-byte chain head, so the leaf comfortably holds both — exercises
# the multi-cell case without dragging in needs_split.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, ''),(2, '')
VERIFY: SELECT n, length(s), substr(s, 1, 1) FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter26.C.3 DELETE oversize row → chain enters freelist; integrity_check
# verifies no orphan pages.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES ('')
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t

# Iter26.C.3 UPDATE shrinking oversize → fits inline. Old chain freed,
# new payload stays on the leaf.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES ('')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'short'
VERIFY: SELECT s, length(s) FROM t

# Iter26.C.3 UPDATE growing inline → oversize. Allocates a fresh chain.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES ('short')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = ''
VERIFY: SELECT length(s), substr(s, 1, 1), substr(s, 5995, 5) FROM t

# Iter26.C: multi-leaf table where one row has an overflow chain.
# sqlite3 SETUP balances ~50 rows across an interior root + 2 leaves
# (one row is oversize so its leaf carries an inline+chain cell).
# sqlite0 must walk the depth-1 tree on SELECT and round-trip the
# chain on a non-matching DELETE.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'padding-row-001-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(2, 'padding-row-002-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(3, 'padding-row-003-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(4, 'padding-row-004-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(5, 'padding-row-005-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(6, 'padding-row-024-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(25, ''),(26, 'padding-row-026-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(27, 'padding-row-027-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(28, 'padding-row-031-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(32, 'padding-row-032-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(33, 'padding-row-033-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(34, 'padding-row-034-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(35, 'padding-row-035-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(36, 'padding-row-036-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(37, 'padding-row-037-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(38, 'padding-row-038-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(39, 'padding-row-039-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(40, 'padding-row-046-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(47, 'padding-row-047-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(48, 'padding-row-048-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(49, 'padding-row-049-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(50, 'padding-row-050-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa')
QUERY: SELECT count(*), length(s) FROM t WHERE n = 25

# Iter26.B.3 depth-2 INSERT. SETUP creates a depth-2 tree (sqlite3
# balances ~600 wide rows into root-interior + multiple interior-children
# + ~600 leaves). MUTATE INSERTs one row, exercising the spine descend
# (root -> interior child -> leaf), leaf split (rightmost leaf is full),
# and bottom-up promote into the rightmost interior child (.fits path).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<600) INSERT INTO t SELECT n, zeroblob(3000) FROM c
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (601, 'tail')
VERIFY: SELECT count(*), max(n) FROM t

# Iter26.B.3 depth-2 UPDATE. Recursive collectLeafPages must walk
# every leaf under every interior child of the depth-2 tree.
# UPDATE re-encodes matched rows in place — neither leaf is left
# empty. (sqlite3 raises CORRUPT on empty leaves under non-root
# parents — the spec only allows empty leaves at the root. Proper
# underfull rebalance is a future iteration; DELETE that would
# wipe a leaf clean is therefore deliberately not exercised here.)
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<600) INSERT INTO t SELECT n, zeroblob(3000) FROM c
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'updated' WHERE n IN (1, 100, 300, 500, 600)
VERIFY: SELECT n, s FROM t WHERE s = 'updated' ORDER BY n

# Iter26.B.3 depth-2 read across all leaves. Tests the recursive
# btree_walk arm that enumerates leaves under each interior child.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<600) INSERT INTO t SELECT n, zeroblob(3000) FROM c
QUERY: SELECT count(*), min(n), max(n), length(s) FROM t WHERE n IN (1, 200, 400, 600)


# ---- Iter27.A: WAL read-side ----
# WAL_SETUP runs after SETUP under `.dbconfig no_ckpt_on_close on`,
# leaving the -wal sidecar populated when sqlite0 opens the file.
# sqlite0 must scan the WAL, populate its in-memory page index, and
# return WAL-resident page versions from getPage so SELECT matches
# sqlite3 (which sees the same WAL on its own subsequent open).

# Single INSERT inside WAL — page 2 (the table root) lives in WAL,
# never made it to main. sqlite0 SELECTing must read it from -wal.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (42);
QUERY: SELECT * FROM t

# Multi-row INSERT in WAL — leaf page is rewritten in-WAL each row.
# Latest-version-wins inside the index because all writes commit.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'one'), (2, 'two'), (3, 'three');
QUERY: SELECT n, s FROM t ORDER BY n

# CREATE TABLE inside WAL — sqlite_schema (page 1) gets a new
# leaf cell appended in WAL. Schema scan at open-time must read
# the WAL-resident page 1, otherwise the table is invisible.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE pre(x); INSERT INTO pre VALUES (1);
WAL_SETUP: CREATE TABLE post(y); INSERT INTO post VALUES (99);
QUERY: SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM pre), (SELECT count(*) FROM post)

# UPDATE in WAL — same page rewritten with new bytes; the WAL frame
# overrides the original page even though both have the same page_no.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3);
WAL_SETUP: UPDATE t SET n = n + 100;
QUERY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# DELETE in WAL — leaf rebuild in-WAL leaves a smaller page.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4), (5);
WAL_SETUP: DELETE FROM t WHERE n % 2 = 0;
QUERY: SELECT count(*), min(n), max(n) FROM t

# Connection-died-mid-tx canary. The unCOMMITted INSERT (999) is not
# guaranteed to reach -wal — sqlite3's page cache may keep it in memory
# until commit forces a frame write. The tentative-then-promoted
# discard path is covered by the synthetic wal_recovery.zig unit test
# that controls bytes on disk directly. This case acts as an
# end-to-end regression canary: whichever way sqlite3 lays out the
# bytes, sqlite0 must agree on the visible rows.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (999);
QUERY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n
